_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: A yield curve displays Treasury yields across maturities from one month to 30 years, with a normal upward slope indicating higher yields for longer maturities due to greater risk and time value. An inverted yield curve, where short-term rates exceed long-term rates, has historically preceded US recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.55%, and the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread is positive at 0.41 percentage points, indicating a return to an upward sloping curve after spending 2022-2024 inverted at 0.0 points, its deepest inversion in the series.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a leading recession indicator, with inversion (negative spread) historically signaling economic downturn risk. The curve was inverted throughout 2022-2024 but has since re-steepened above zero to 0.41 points, suggesting the immediate recession signal may have diminished. Additionally, the NY Fed recession probability is currently 31%, reflecting elevated but not extreme recession risk. It is important to note that the curve often un-inverts shortly before recessions begin, so the positive spread does not eliminate the possibility of a recession.
notes.now: The current positive 2s10s spread of 0.41 points, combined with a 31% NY Fed recession probability, indicates a moderate economic risk environment where the yield curve suggests reduced, but still noteworthy, recession concerns. The un-inversion of the curve suggests that the prior strong recession signal has softened, though risk remains elevated relative to historical norms.
